critic_raw:
The GNN operates on 'interaction graphs' where nodes are users and/or content and edges are interactions. A stratified random split on nodes or edges of a graph is not the same as random sampling of IID observations. When a user node is placed in the test set but its interaction neighbors are in the training set, the test-node's graph-neighborhood is fully observed during training. The GNN learns embedding representations that propagate information from training nodes to test nodes through shared edges. This is transductive leakage: the model has effectively seen the test nodes during training via message passing over shared edges. The evaluation is measuring transductive performance, not inductive generalization, and the design never addresses this distinction.

AUROC is described as appropriate for 'rank-ordering of engagement predictions.' But engagement on an educational platform is defined as a binary variable (engaged vs. not engaged), and AUROC on binary engagement may be heavily influenced by class imbalance if positive engagement is the majority. More critically, the hypothesis is about 'interaction depth and content relevance'—neither of which is captured by a single binary engagement label. The hypothesis is richer than the target variable.

The logistic regression baseline receives 'identical features transformed into vector format.' For a GNN comparison to be meaningful, the baseline must either (a) also use relational features derived from the graph to test if graph structure adds value beyond node attributes, or (b) use only node-level features to test if the GNN's architectural advantage justifies its complexity. The design conflates these two questions by giving the logistic regression 'the same features in vector format'—which collapses graph structure into flat features and confounds representation with model architecture.

Seasonal trends and marketing campaigns are mentioned as confounds mitigated through 'temporal adjustment controls.' But in an online learning platform, the most important temporal confound is the temporal curriculum structure: early content is simpler and engages more users; harder content has lower engagement independent of model predictions. A random split will mix early-course and late-course content in both train and test, allowing the model to learn course-position effects rather than genuine engagement signals.

The dataset spans two years, but online learning platforms evolve their content, UI, and recommendation algorithms over that period. Pooling two years of interaction data under a random split means the model trains on behavioral responses to a recommendation algorithm that was itself changing; any improvement over logistic regression may reflect the GNN's ability to model the recommendation algorithm's historical decisions, not student engagement with content.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on graph data causes transductive leakage: test nodes share edges with training nodes, so the GNN observes test nodes during message passing and the evaluation measures transductive memorization rather than inductive generalization.

The binary engagement label does not operationalize 'interaction depth and content relevance' as stated in the hypothesis, making the experiment unable to verify the mechanism the hypothesis proposes.

The logistic regression baseline receives graph features flattened to vectors, conflating graph structure representation with model architecture; the design cannot isolate whether the GNN's advantage comes from relational modeling or simply from richer features.

Temporal curriculum structure (early vs. late course content having different baseline engagement rates) is not controlled; the random split mixes curriculum positions in train and test, allowing the model to learn position effects rather than engagement signals.

The platform's recommendation algorithm changed over the two-year pooled period; the GNN may be learning to model the historical recommendation algorithm's outputs rather than genuine student engagement with content.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on graph data causes transductive leakage: test nodes share edges with training nodes, so the GNN observes test nodes during message passing and the evaluation measures transductive memorization rather than inductive generalization.

The binary engagement label does not operationalize 'interaction depth and content relevance' as stated in the hypothesis, making the experiment unable to verify the mechanism the hypothesis proposes.

The logistic regression baseline receives graph features flattened to vectors, conflating graph structure representation with model architecture; the design cannot isolate whether the GNN's advantage comes from relational modeling or simply from richer features.

Temporal curriculum structure (early vs. late course content having different baseline engagement rates) is not controlled; the random split mixes curriculum positions in train and test, allowing the model to learn position effects rather than engagement signals.

The platform's recommendation algorithm changed over the two-year pooled period; the GNN may be learning to model the historical recommendation algorithm's outputs rather than genuine student engagement with content.
